## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

    Add `include::` lines to BUS0 at appropriate positions.

    - AXLA (`Tools/cmk/vov_veiled/AXLA-Lexicon.adoc`) — motif vocabulary and voicing annotations
    - MCM (`Tools/cmk/vov_veiled/MCM-MetaConceptModel.adoc`) — linked term structure: mapping section, anchors, definition sites
    - BUSD-DispatchRuntime.adoc — existing BUS0 subdocument precedent
    
    **Phase 2 — Write three subdocuments:**
    
    Architecture context (resolved in ₢A-AAC): `zburh_build_key_line` in `burh_regime.sh` is the shared pure string builder (sets `ZBURH_KEY_LINE` and `ZBURH_KEY_MARKER`). Platform wrappers handle I/O: Unix file ops on Linux/Mac, PowerShell on Windows. Marker format `# BURH:<alias>` is the idempotency wire contract.
    
    Three commands to specify:
    1. `burh_install_key` (buw-rhk) — Linux/Mac key install. Uses `${HOME}/.ssh/authorized_keys`, chmod 600.
    2. `buwc_install_key` (buw-wck) — Windows admin key install. Uses `ZBUHW_ADMIN_AUTH_KEYS` (PowerShell I/O), icacls for permissions.
    3. `buwc_bootstrap_sshd` (buw-wcb) — Windows sshd provisioning. Platform gate (WSL required), `buc_require "OVERWRITE"` confirmation gate. Owns sshd_config, service, firewall. Does NOT touch authorized_keys.
    
    Each subdocument should specify:
    - Inputs (regime variables consumed, folio requirements)
    - Preconditions (platform gates, confirmation gates)
    - Behavior (what it writes, idempotency guarantees)
    - Ownership boundary (what it does and does NOT manage)
    - Wire formats (marker pattern for key install commands)
    
    Add `include::` lines to BUS0 at appropriate positions.

[2] rough (basis: 7fc79f3)
    Silks: specify-new-buk-commands-in-bus0

    ## Character
    Spec writing — fleshing out a stub spec into full ceremony coverage. Mostly mechanical once Garrison/Conscript phase boundaries are clear.
    
    ## Docket
    Elaborate `Tools/buk/vov_veiled/BUSNW-NodeWindows.adoc` covering Garrison and Conscript ceremonies on Windows.
    
    Garrison phases: console (operator at keyboard, handbook surface only), handshake (first SSH via temp creds), provisioning (sshd_config, service, firewall, admin authorized_keys), harden (disable temp auth, verify keys-only).
    
    Conscript phases per workload shell: remote user create via privileged BURN, per-user authorized_keys (NOT admin auth_keys), workload `burn.env` write, verification.
    
    Wire format details: `administrators_authorized_keys` path + `icacls` ACL contract; per-user authorized_keys path; sshd_config directives Windows OpenSSH refuses (`UsePAM`, `ChallengeResponseAuthentication`).
    
    Codify the PowerShell-from-WSL pattern: bash owns control flow, PowerShell is the Windows syscall layer, one atomic command per call.
    
    Original AAQ scope morphed: per-command subdocs no longer the right shape under the tier model. Per-platform subdoc is the right home.

# jjezs_paddock ₣A-

## Goal

Windows test infrastructure for Recipe Bottle. Run RB's container isolation
tests on a Windows host with WSL, Cygwin, and dual Docker daemons.

## Current Design

The architectural backbone lives in BUS0 §Remote Node Access (`Tools/buk/vov_veiled/BUS0-BashUtilitiesSpec.adoc`)
plus three platform subdocs (`BUSNW-NodeWindows.adoc`, `BUSNL-NodeLinux.adoc`,
`BUSNM-NodeMac.adoc`). Read BUS0 §Remote Node Access first; this paddock
captures only state that doesn't fit there.

**Header decisions made in this heat:**

- BURH regime renamed to BURN (n = Node)
- BURN_TIER field added: `privileged | workload`
- Two-tier model:
  - Privileged tier: node-level ops (sshd config, user provisioning).
    Multi-resident allowed; one operator at a time by social contract.
  - Workload tier: user-level ops (normal remote work). Exclusive to one
    station user; ephemeral.
- Verb vocabulary (BUSN quoins): Garrison / Conscript / Discharge / Inventory
- Tabtarget colophon families under `buw-`:
  - `buw-rn{l,r,v}` — BURN regime ops (list / render / validate)
  - `buw-rnc` — SSH config aggregator (cross-tier, all profiles)
  - `buw-npg{l,m,c,w,p,x}` — Garrison per platform (privileged setup)
  - `buw-nwc{l,m,c,w,p,x}` — Conscript per platform (workload mint)
  - `buw-nw{d,i}` — Discharge / Inventory
  - `buw-nw{c,r,s}` — Check / Run / Ssh (workload operational)
  - `buw-hn0` — Handbook landing for node ops
- Visual proximity note: `buw-nwc` (Check) vs `buw-nwc{platform}` (Conscript)
  — distinct colophons, accepted

## Heat Sequence (post-triage and post-audit)

Spec backbone in place; spec subdocs need elaboration; implementation paces
slated; practice unchanged.

**Spec phase (pending):**

- ₢A-AAR specify-zburn-key-line-builder (reslated; BUWC half dropped)
- ₢A-AAQ elaborate-busnw-windows-ceremony (reslated; morphed from
  per-command subdocs)
- ₢A-AAU specify-busn-tier-refusal (newly slated)

**Implementation phase (newly slated, sequenced):**

- ₢A-AAV burh-to-burn-rename-buk (mechanical)
- ₢A-AAW burh-to-burn-rename-jjk (mechanical, depends on AAV)
- ₢A-AAX garrison-platform-machinery (refactor + new code, six platforms)
- ₢A-AAY conscript-discharge-inventory-machinery (workload-tier mirror,
  depends on AAX)
- ₢A-AAZ workload-operational-tabtargets (Check / Run / Ssh)
- ₢A-AAa node-handbook-landing-and-windows-residue (handbook restructure,
  depends on AAX and AAY)
- ₢A-AAb buw-rnc-ssh-config-aggregator (colophon move; may fold into AAV)

**Practice phase:**

- ₢A-AAC (reslated for new verbs) — practice with Garrison/Conscript on
  real hardware
- ₢A-AAD WSL + Cygwin install (unchanged)
- ₢A-AAE fundus provisioning (unchanged)
- ₢A-AAF Docker dual-daemon (unchanged)

**Regression:**

- ₢A-AAP retest fundus after practice (unchanged)

Triaged out (no slate change; act on at wrap time):

- ₢A-AAH (verification tabtargets) — fold into practice wrap notes
- ₢A-AAI (reconsider Cygwin install) — fold into AAD wrap notes
- ₢A-AAJ (autonumber sweep) — relocate to ₣A6
- ₢A-AAS, ₢A-AAT (handbook-render fixture coverage) — relocate to ₣BB

## Deferred (named for future return)

- **Adopt** — cross-station-user pubkey install via existing privileged
  trust. Today: each station user runs the ceremony fresh from their
  own machine.
- **User regime** — centralized per-user pubkey for shared admin
  scenarios. Pairs naturally with Adopt when both are needed.
- **Git normalization on remote** — JJK has `jjx_plant`; BUK doesn't
  duplicate.

## Standing Notes

- Tailscale provides stable transport (Mac sees `rocket` as
  `100.x.y.z` / tailnet hostname); not adopted as a BUK dependency
- Windows username `b hyslop` (with space) — audit shell quoting at
  every `${BURN_USER}` expansion during practice
- Fundus capability registry: `Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSFR-FundusRegistry.md`
- PowerShell-from-WSL pattern: bash owns control flow, PowerShell is
  the Windows syscall layer; one atomic command per call
- BURS_USER station regime field routes profiles to
  `.buk/users/${BURS_USER}/` subdirectory
- Three Windows BURN profiles for `rocket` distinguished by
  `BURN_COMMAND`: cygwin (`...bash.exe -l`), WSL (`wsl.exe -d ...`),
  PowerShell (`powershell.exe`)

=== Windows Node Access

Platform-specific ceremony and contracts for Windows node access.

==== Status

Stub.
Elaborate content to be drafted by subsequent paces — Windows is the
canonical worked example for the Remote Node Access ceremony, requiring
detailed coverage of OpenSSH server provisioning, the
`administrators_authorized_keys` special case, ACL fixes via `icacls`,
and the temp-password bootstrap handshake.

==== Privilege Contract

The {burn_user} of a Windows privileged BURN must be a member of the local
Administrators group.
Rationale: Windows OpenSSH consults `C:\ProgramData\ssh\administrators_authorized_keys`
for users in the Administrators group, ignoring the per-user
`~/.ssh/authorized_keys` for those users.
A non-admin path would require parallel implementation with no return for
the supported use cases.

Asserted at every ceremony participant before any modification.

==== Platform Letters

* `c` — Cygwin (`buw-npgc`, `buw-nwcc`)
* `w` — WSL (`buw-npgw`, `buw-nwcw`)
* `p` — PowerShell (`buw-npgp`, `buw-nwcp`)

==== Out of Band

The console phase of the Windows ceremony is performed at the Windows
physical keyboard (or RDP) by the operator and is described in the BUK
handbook surface, not in this spec.

== Remote Node Access

Bringing remote nodes under SSH key control from a local station, establishing
trust that supports both privileged node-level operations and ephemeral
workload-tier work over the same {burn_regime}.

=== Privilege Tiers

The {burn_tier} field of every {burn_regime} profile classifies the profile
as either `privileged` or `workload`. Tabtargets refuse aliases of the wrong
tier at entry.

==== Privileged Tier

Privileged profiles authenticate as a remote OS user with admin or equivalent
privileges. Used for node-level operations: sshd configuration, user
provisioning, node-establishment ceremonies.

Multiple station users may hold privileged trust on the same node (file
format supports multi-resident keys). Operational convention: only one
station user actively orchestrates work at a time. This convention is
enforced by social contract, not BUK locking.

==== Workload Tier

Workload profiles authenticate as ephemeral remote OS users created by
privileged authority. Used for normal remote work: running commands,
file operations, interactive shells.

Workload users are exclusive to one station user — not handed off between
station users. Lifecycle is short:
{busn_conscript}
when needed,
{busn_discharge}
when done.

=== Tabtarget Catalog

Tabtargets under `buw-` consume {burn_regime} profiles to perform remote
operations. Tier-specific colophons follow `buw-n[pw]` for clarity.

==== Lifecycle

`buw-rnl`::
List all {burn_regime} profiles in the local station subtree.

`buw-rnr <alias>`::
Render a single {burn_regime} profile.

`buw-rnv <alias>`::
Validate a single {burn_regime} profile.

==== Privileged (per-platform node setup)

`buw-npg{l,m,c,w,p,x} <host> <user> <moniker>`::
{busn_garrison} a node for the named platform.
Writes the privileged {burn_regime} profile and runs the platform-specific
ceremony to establish admin trust.
Platform letters: l=Linux, m=Mac, c=Cygwin, w=WSL, p=PowerShell, x=Localhost.

==== Workload (privileged authority required)

`buw-nwc{l,m,c,w,p,x} <privileged-alias> <workload-moniker>`::
{busn_conscript} a workload user via the named privileged BURN.
Creates the remote OS user, installs the station user's pubkey, and
writes a workload {burn_regime} profile locally.

`buw-nwd <workload-alias>`::
{busn_discharge} a workload user.
Deletes the remote OS user via the workload profile's privileged
authority and removes the local profile.

`buw-nwi <privileged-alias>`::
{busn_inventory} workload users present on the node, queried via the
privileged BURN.

==== Workload (operational)

`buw-nwc <workload-alias>`::
Check responsiveness via passwordless SSH probe.

`buw-nwr <workload-alias> <cmd-file>`::
Run the contents of `<cmd-file>` on the remote node; capture output to
{burd_output_dir}.

`buw-nws <workload-alias>`::
Open an interactive SSH session.

==== Handbook

`buw-hn0`::
Top-level handbook landing for node access procedures.

=== Privileges

The {burs_user} owns its profile subtree at `.buk/users/{burs_user}/`
exclusively — operations write only under the current station user's tree.

A {burn_regime} profile describes a connection identity (host, user, shell,
alias, key, tier) — not project state, not work-directory. Project context
is supplied by the consumer at invocation time.

Each platform subdoc declares the remote-user privilege contract that its
ceremony assumes (e.g., on Windows the privileged user must be a member of
the Administrators group).

=== Verb Definitions

[[busn_garrison]]
//axl_voices axt_command
{busn_garrison}::
Establish privileged BURN trust on a node by writing the privileged
{burn_regime} profile and running the platform-specific ceremony to gain
admin access. Per-platform tabtargets at `buw-npg{platform}`. First-time
node setup; idempotent on re-run.

[[busn_conscript]]
//axl_voices axt_command
{busn_conscript}::
Create an ephemeral workload user on a node and provision a workload
{burn_regime} profile locally. Requires a privileged BURN as authority for
the remote-user creation. Per-platform tabtargets at `buw-nwc{platform}`,
where the platform letter selects the workload shell — independent of the
privileged BURN's shell choice.

[[busn_discharge]]
//axl_voices axt_command
{busn_discharge}::
Remove a workload user from a node and delete the local workload
{burn_regime} profile. The workload profile encodes its privileged
authority; {busn_discharge} uses that authority to delete the remote OS
user. Tabtarget: `buw-nwd`.

[[busn_inventory]]
//axl_voices axt_command
{busn_inventory}::
List workload users currently provisioned on a node, queried via a
privileged BURN. Scope is the node's full workload-user population — not
just the current station user's history. Tabtarget: `buw-nwi`.

include::BUSNW-NodeWindows.adoc[]

include::BUSNL-NodeLinux.adoc[]

include::BUSNM-NodeMac.adoc[]

==== Status

Stub.
Linux ceremony content to be drafted when first Linux fundus target is
brought online.
The expected ceremony is substantially shorter than Windows because most
Linux distributions ship with OpenSSH server pre-configured and accept

To be specified.

Specific ACL or directory service configuration to be specified when the
contract is actually exercised.

* `m` — macOS (`buw-npgm`, `buw-nwcm`)

==== Out of Band

To be specified.

content.text:
Here's my read.

**Current docket (rough [3]) summary:** Elaborate the 36-line stub `BUSNW-NodeWindows.adoc` with Garrison phases (console/handshake/provisioning/harden), Conscript per workload shell, wire formats (admin_authorized_keys, icacls, sshd_config refusals), and the PowerShell-from-WSL pattern. The docket is already on its third reslate after pivoting from per-command to per-platform shape.

**Three things I'd call out as worth a revision conversation:**

1. **Pre-baked phase names.** "console, handshake, provisioning, harden" is a structural decision mount-time would normally make. Looking at the paddock's "Header decisions made in this heat" section, the verb vocabulary (Garrison/Conscript/Discharge/Inventory) is locked, but Garrison's internal phase decomposition is not. Either those phase names should move up into the paddock as a settled decision, or come out of the docket so mount-time owns the call. JJK's anti-pattern guidance flags exactly this ("Pre-baking implementation ... Mount-time decisions").

2. **Boundary with sibling spec paces.** AAR specifies the `zburn_build_key_line` builder. AAU specifies tier refusal. AAQ owns Windows ceremony. But AAQ's wire-format list includes `administrators_authorized_keys` path + icacls — is that AAQ (Windows install target) or AAR (key install)? And tier-refusal mention sneaks into Garrison's "ownership boundary" too. A one-line "AAQ owns X, defers Y to AAR/AAU" would prevent overlap at mount.

3. **"Conscript phases per workload shell"** — Conscript per workload shell isn't really phased; it's one shape with per-shell variation (what shell gets installed for the workload user). Phrasing as "phases" invites mount-time to invent structure that isn't there.

**Smaller nits:** the PowerShell-from-WSL pattern is cross-cutting (any Windows access uses it), but the standing-notes paddock already calls it out — housing it in BUSNW as the canonical Windows worked example is defensible, just worth being explicit about.

Want me to draft a revised docket addressing #1 and #2, or talk through what should be in the paddock first?

## Paces

### elaborate-busnw-windows-ceremony (₢A-AAQ) [rough]

## Character
Spec writing — fleshing out a stub spec into full ceremony coverage. Mostly mechanical once Garrison/Conscript phase boundaries are clear.

## Docket
Elaborate `Tools/buk/vov_veiled/BUSNW-NodeWindows.adoc` covering Garrison and Conscript ceremonies on Windows.

Garrison phases: console (operator at keyboard, handbook surface only), handshake (first SSH via temp creds), provisioning (sshd_config, service, firewall, admin authorized_keys), harden (disable temp auth, verify keys-only).

Conscript phases per workload shell: remote user create via privileged BURN, per-user authorized_keys (NOT admin auth_keys), workload `burn.env` write, verification.

Wire format details: `administrators_authorized_keys` path + `icacls` ACL contract; per-user authorized_keys path; sshd_config directives Windows OpenSSH refuses (`UsePAM`, `ChallengeResponseAuthentication`).

Codify the PowerShell-from-WSL pattern: bash owns control flow, PowerShell is the Windows syscall layer, one atomic command per call.

Original AAQ scope morphed: per-command subdocs no longer the right shape under the tier model. Per-platform subdoc is the right home.

### burh-to-burn-rename-buk (₢A-AAV) [rough]

## Character
Mechanical sweep — rename across BUK, no semantic change. Absorbs former AAU (tier-refusal helper) and AAb (SSH config aggregator colophon move).

## Docket
Wholesale rename BURH → BURN across BUK: variables, function prefixes, module file names, profile filename (`burh.env` → `burn.env`), zipper enrollments, tabtarget filenames. Add `BURN_TIER` enrollment (`privileged|workload`); the field exists, enforcement helper lands here.

Constructor enrollments (`BUWZ_RHC_*`) and Windows command enrollments (`BUWZ_WC_*`) keep names provisionally; they retire in Garrison/Conscript paces, not here.

Tier-refusal helper: mint a centralized assertion (e.g., `burn_assert_tier <expected> <alias>`) that compares the alias's `BURN_TIER` against the expected tier. Error format: name expected tier, observed tier, alias name; exit non-zero before any side effect. BUS0 already states the contract on `burn_tier`; this codifies the helper and its error shape so Garrison/Conscript paces consume one implementation.

SSH config aggregator move: rename `buw-HWsc.SshConfig.sh` → `buw-rnc.NodeSshConfig.sh` and update its underlying function/module names. Tier-aware filtering: aggregator emits `~/.ssh/config` entries for both tiers (privileged and workload); document the choice. Update handbook references that pointed at the old colophon.

Validation: `tt/buw-st.BukSelfTest.sh` and BUK-touching fixtures pass.

Discovery: `grep -rln 'BURH\|burh' Tools/buk/ tt/`.

### burh-to-burn-rename-jjk (₢A-AAW) [rough]

## Character
Mechanical sweep — rename across JJK Rust + spec, no semantic change.

## Docket
Wholesale rename BURH → BURN across JJK: env-var reads, `burh.env` path → `burn.env`, identifier renames, spec text, claude-context.

# jjezs_paddock ₣A-

## Goal

Windows test infrastructure for Recipe Bottle. Run RB's container isolation
tests on a Windows host with WSL, Cygwin, and dual Docker daemons.

## Current Design

The architectural backbone lives in BUS0 §Remote Node Access (`Tools/buk/vov_veiled/BUS0-BashUtilitiesSpec.adoc`)
plus three platform subdocs (`BUSNW-NodeWindows.adoc`, `BUSNL-NodeLinux.adoc`,
`BUSNM-NodeMac.adoc`). Read BUS0 §Remote Node Access first; this paddock
captures only state that doesn't fit there.

**Header decisions made in this heat:**

- BURH regime renamed to BURN (n = Node) — decision; execution in AAV/AAW
- BURN_TIER field added: `privileged | workload`
- Two-tier model:
  - Privileged tier: node-level ops (sshd config, user provisioning).
    Multi-resident allowed; one operator at a time by social contract.
  - Workload tier: user-level ops (normal remote work). Exclusive to one
    station user; ephemeral.
- Verb vocabulary (BUSN quoins): Garrison / Conscript / Discharge / Inventory
- Tabtarget colophon families under `buw-`:
  - `buw-rn{l,r,v}` — BURN regime ops (list / render / validate)
  - `buw-rnc` — SSH config aggregator (cross-tier, all profiles)
  - `buw-npg{l,m,c,w,p,x}` — Garrison per platform (privileged setup)
  - `buw-nwc{l,m,c,w,p,x}` — Conscript per platform (workload mint)
  - `buw-nw{d,i}` — Discharge / Inventory
  - `buw-nw{c,r,s}` — Check / Run / Ssh (workload operational)
  - `buw-hn0` — Handbook landing for node ops
- Visual proximity note: `buw-nwc` (Check) vs `buw-nwc{platform}` (Conscript)
  — distinct colophons, accepted

## Heat Sequence

**Spec phase (pending):**

- ₢A-AAQ elaborate-busnw-windows-ceremony — flesh BUSNW from stub to full
  Garrison/Conscript ceremony coverage on Windows

**Implementation phase (sequenced):**

- ₢A-AAV burh-to-burn-rename-buk — mechanical rename + tier-refusal helper
  + SSH config aggregator move (`buw-HWsc` → `buw-rnc`)
- ₢A-AAW burh-to-burn-rename-jjk — mechanical, depends on AAV
- ₢A-AAX garrison-platform-machinery — six platforms; defines key-line
  wire format (`# BURN:<alias>` marker) inline
- ₢A-AAY conscript-discharge-inventory-machinery — workload-tier mirror,

**Goal:** Windows test infrastructure for RB — RB's container isolation tests on a Windows host with WSL, Cygwin, dual Docker daemons.

**Direct answer to your question — paces that build out the BUS0 tabtargets and implement BURN bash:**

| Pace | Silks | What it owns |
|------|-------|--------------|
| **₢A-AAV** | burh-to-burn-rename-buk | Mechanical BURH→BURN sweep across BUK; adds `BURN_TIER` enrollment + `burn_assert_tier` helper; renames `buw-HWsc` → `buw-rnc` (SSH config aggregator move) |
| **₢A-AAW** | burh-to-burn-rename-jjk | Same rename across JJK Rust + spec; depends on AAV |
| **₢A-AAX** | garrison-platform-machinery | Implements `buw-npg{l,m,c,w,p,x}` (six platforms); defines `# BURN:<alias>` key-line wire format inline; retires `buw-rhc{...}`, `buw-wcb`, `buw-wck` |
| **₢A-AAY** | conscript-discharge-inventory-machinery | Implements `busn_conscript_{l,m,c,w,p,x}`, `busn_discharge`, `busn_inventory`; depends on AAX |
| **₢A-AAZ** | workload-operational-tabtargets | Implements `buw-nw{c,r,s}` (Check/Run/Ssh); extracts Check from existing `burn_verify_ssh` |
| **₢A-AAa** | node-handbook-landing-and-windows-residue | Implements `buw-hn0` landing; mints console-handshake residue tabtarget; retires access trio (`buw-HWab/ar/ax`); depends on AAX + AAY |

That's the full implementation block: AAV → AAW → AAX → AAY → AAZ → AAa, with sequencing constraints noted.

**On AAQ (your real question):**

I agree it doesn't carry weight as currently shaped. Three reasons:

1. **AAX has already absorbed the wire-format definition.** Look at AAX's docket: "*Key-line wire format (the former AAR scope, now inline)... Keep the format definition co-located with the only producer.*" The same logic applies to admin_authorized_keys path, icacls contract, sshd_config refusals — they're consumed by AAX/AAa, and inline-with-producer beats spec-ahead.

2. **The platform subdoc precedent is "draft when first exercised."** BUSNL and BUSNM both say "stub... to be drafted when first Linux/macOS fundus target is brought online." That same posture suits BUSNW: draft after AAC practice exposes what actually matters, not before.

3. **The spec backbone is already in place.** BUS0 §Remote Node Access has Garrison/Conscript/Discharge/Inventory verbs defined with tabtarget catalog + verb definitions + privilege contract. BUSNW's current 36 lines cover what mount-time needs: privilege contract, platform letters, Out-of-Band note. The "elaboration" is largely re-articulation.

**Three options for AAQ:**

- **Drop it.** Implementation paces (AAX/AAY/AAa) carry their own wire-format scope inline; BUSNW gets retroactive elaboration if/when AAC practice teaches us something the spec needs to lock down. Move AAQ to retired or itch.
- **Defer it.** Keep AAQ slated but move it after AAC practice — "elaborate BUSNW with what practice taught us." Becomes an evidence-driven spec pace, not a speculative one.
- **Shrink it.** Keep AAQ but narrow scope to the Windows-only contracts that genuinely cross paces (e.g., the privilege contract is already there; what else?). Probably this collapses to "no work needed" once you list it.

My lean: **defer (option 2)**. Renaming spec elaboration as a consolidation step after practice is honest about what specs are for here.

What would you like to do?

content.text:
Good instinct. Two thoughts that strengthen it, and one question.

**Symmetry concern: same logic applies to AAX.**

AAX currently mints six platforms too. Look at its docket: "*Linux/Mac/Localhost: minimal — assume existing sshd, key install via privileged authority. Windows variants (Cygwin/WSL/PowerShell): full ceremony — sshd bootstrap, admin authorized_keys install with `command=` routing per shell.*" The non-Windows variants are speculative until those fundus targets come online — same situation as conscript. If you split AAY by platform, the L/M/X garrison code in AAX is equally unexercised weight.

I'd recommend splitting both, or splitting neither. My lean: split both Windows-first.

**"Windows conscript" is three variants, not one.**

Conscript per workload shell means `busn_conscript_{c,w,p}` — Cygwin, WSL, PowerShell. They share the user-creation primitive (`net user` or `New-LocalUser` via the privileged BURN's PowerShell-from-WSL syscall layer); only the workload shell selection differs. They should stay together in one pace. Discharge and Inventory follow the same logic — Windows-only now means Windows-side `net user /delete` + `net user` listing.

**Question: should "later" be a pace or an itch?**

Per JJK vocabulary, an itch is future work whose timing is uncertain. Linux/Mac/Localhost garrison-conscript only matters when there's a first L/M fundus target — a condition that may never materialize for this heat's purposes. Slating it as a pace implies commitment; capturing it as an itch admits "we'll mint these variants when the need lands." I'd lean itch over pace.

**Concretely, what I'd propose:**

| Pace | Scope |
|------|-------|
| AAV | (unchanged) BURH→BURN rename in BUK |
| AAW | (unchanged) BURH→BURN rename in JJK |
| **AAX** (reslate) | garrison-windows-machinery — `buw-npg{c,w,p}` only; defers L/M/X to itch |
| **AAY** (reslate) | conscript-discharge-inventory-windows — `busn_conscript_{c,w,p}` + Windows-side discharge/inventory |
| AAZ | (unchanged) workload-operational-tabtargets |
| AAa | (unchanged) handbook landing + windows residue |
| **new itch** | garrison-conscript-other-platforms — mint L/M/X variants when first non-Windows fundus target arrives |

Does that match what you had in mind, or do you want the L/M/X variants to be a real slated pace (committed but later) rather than an itch?

content.text:
**On railing implementation first:**

Right call. Three reasons converge:

1. **The spec backbone is already there.** BUS0 §Remote Node Access has the verbs, catalog, contracts, and tier model. AAQ's "elaborate BUSNW" is downstream documentation of platform-specific contracts that don't exist as code yet. You can't faithfully spec what code does before code exists.

2. **AAX/AAY were already going to inline the wire formats.** AAX's docket explicitly absorbs the key-line format ("former AAR scope, now inline; keep co-located with the only producer"). The same logic claims admin_authorized_keys, icacls, sshd_config refusals — they belong with their producer. AAQ was going to spec contracts the implementation paces own.

3. **Practice (AAC) is the test of truth.** Anything BUSNW eventually says about Windows ceremony either survives practice or it's wrong. Writing it before practice makes it speculative; writing it after makes it evidence-driven (and probably much shorter).

Implementation-first naturally retires AAQ — either dropped entirely, or reborn as a tiny post-AAC consolidation. I'd retire it now and let AAC tell us whether BUSNW needs anything beyond its current 36 lines.

Now the splits.

# jjezs_reslate A-AAX

## Character
Implementation — Windows-only garrison. Mint per-shell ceremony for the three Windows variants (Cygwin/WSL/PowerShell). Defines the key-line wire format inline.

## Docket
Mint `busn_garrison` for the three Windows shells per BUS0 catalog: `buw-npg{c,w,p}`. Each writes a privileged `burn.env` (BURN_TIER=privileged) and runs the Windows ceremony — sshd bootstrap, admin authorized_keys install with `command=` routing per shell. Console phase is handbook-surface residue (AAa), not Garrison code.

Key-line wire format (former AAR scope, now inline): `zburn_build_key_line` produces `[command="...",options ]<keytype> <base64> # BURN:<alias>`. The trailing `# BURN:<alias>` is the idempotency marker that key-install and SSH-config-aggregation grep for. `command=` prefix is conditional on the platform's routing needs. Keep the format definition co-located with the only producer.

Retire `buw-rhc{c,w,p}`, `buw-wcb`, `buw-wck` files and zipper enrollments.

Linux/Mac/Localhost garrison variants (`buw-npg{l,m,x}`) are out of scope and not slated elsewhere — they do not exist after this pace. Disposition of legacy `buw-rhc{l,m,x}` (BURN-renamed in AAV but no garrison replacement): mount-time decision based on whether they're still consumed by anything (e.g., localhost fundus scenarios). Update BUS0 §Tabtarget Catalog to reflect the actual extent (`buw-npg{c,w,p}`) so the spec describes what exists.

Tier assertion via the helper minted in AAV: Garrison rejects workload aliases.

# jjezs_reslate A-AAY

## Character
Implementation — Windows-only workload-tier mirror of Garrison. Three workload-shell variants share user-creation logic; Windows-side discharge/inventory.

## Docket
Mint per BUS0 catalog (Windows scope):

`busn_conscript_{c,w,p}` (`buw-nwc{c,w,p}`) — create remote Windows OS user via privileged BURN (`net user` / `New-LocalUser` via PowerShell-from-WSL), install station-user pubkey to per-user `authorized_keys` (NOT admin path), write workload `burn.env` (BURN_TIER=workload). Workload shell choice (c/w/p) independent of privileged BURN's shell.

`busn_discharge` (`buw-nwd`) — use the workload profile's recorded privileged-authority back-reference to delete the remote Windows user; remove local profile.

`busn_inventory` (`buw-nwi`) — list workload users on a Windows node via privileged BURN. Scope: full workload-user population.

New BURN field: workload profiles record their privileged-authority alias.

Linux/Mac/Localhost conscript variants (`buw-nwc{l,m,x}`) and their discharge/inventory cousins are out of scope and not slated elsewhere — they do not exist after this pace. Update BUS0 §Tabtarget Catalog to reflect the actual extent (`buw-nwc{c,w,p}`).

Tier assertions: privileged-alias args reject workload tier; workload-alias args reject privileged tier.

# jjezs_paddock ₣A-

## Goal

Windows test infrastructure for Recipe Bottle. Run RB's container isolation
tests on a Windows host with WSL, Cygwin, and dual Docker daemons.

## Current Design

The architectural backbone lives in BUS0 §Remote Node Access (`Tools/buk/vov_veiled/BUS0-BashUtilitiesSpec.adoc`)
plus three platform subdocs (`BUSNW-NodeWindows.adoc`, `BUSNL-NodeLinux.adoc`,
`BUSNM-NodeMac.adoc`). Read BUS0 §Remote Node Access first; this paddock
captures only state that doesn't fit there.

**Header decisions made in this heat:**

- BURH regime renamed to BURN (n = Node) — decision; execution in AAV/AAW
- BURN_TIER field added: `privileged | workload`
- Two-tier model:
  - Privileged tier: node-level ops (sshd config, user provisioning).
    Multi-resident allowed; one operator at a time by social contract.
  - Workload tier: user-level ops (normal remote work). Exclusive to one
    station user; ephemeral.
- Verb vocabulary (BUSN quoins): Garrison / Conscript / Discharge / Inventory
- Tabtarget colophon families under `buw-`:
  - `buw-rn{l,r,v}` — BURN regime ops (list / render / validate)
  - `buw-rnc` — SSH config aggregator (cross-tier, all profiles)
  - `buw-npg{l,m,c,w,p,x}` — Garrison per platform (privileged setup)
  - `buw-nwc{l,m,c,w,p,x}` — Conscript per platform (workload mint)
  - `buw-nw{d,i}` — Discharge / Inventory
  - `buw-nw{c,r,s}` — Check / Run / Ssh (workload operational)
  - `buw-hn0` — Handbook landing for node ops
- Visual proximity note: `buw-nwc` (Check) vs `buw-nwc{platform}` (Conscript)
  — distinct colophons, accepted

## Heat Sequence

**Spec phase (pending):**

- ₢A-AAQ elaborate-busnw-windows-ceremony — flesh BUSNW from stub to full
  Garrison/Conscript ceremony coverage on Windows

**Implementation phase (sequenced):**

- ₢A-AAV burh-to-burn-rename-buk — mechanical rename + tier-refusal helper
  + SSH config aggregator move (`buw-HWsc` → `buw-rnc`)
- ₢A-AAW burh-to-burn-rename-jjk — mechanical, depends on AAV
- ₢A-AAX garrison-platform-machinery — six platforms; defines key-line
  wire format (`# BURN:<alias>` marker) inline
- ₢A-AAY conscript-discharge-inventory-machinery — workload-tier mirror,
  depends on AAX
- ₢A-AAZ workload-operational-tabtargets — Check / Run / Ssh
- ₢A-AAa node-handbook-landing-and-windows-residue — handbook restructure,
  depends on AAX and AAY

**Practice phase:**

- ₢A-AAC — practice with Garrison/Conscript on real hardware
- ₢A-AAD — WSL + Cygwin install
- ₢A-AAE — fundus provisioning
- ₢A-AAF — Docker dual-daemon

**Regression:**

- ₢A-AAP retest fundus after practice

## Deferred (named for future return)

- **Adopt** — cross-station-user pubkey install via existing privileged
  trust. Today: each station user runs the ceremony fresh from their
  own machine.
- **User regime** — centralized per-user pubkey for shared admin
  scenarios. Pairs naturally with Adopt when both are needed.
- **Git normalization on remote** — JJK has `jjx_plant`; BUK doesn't
  duplicate.

## Standing Notes

- Tailscale provides stable transport (Mac sees `rocket` as
  `100.x.y.z` / tailnet hostname); not adopted as a BUK dependency
- Windows username `b hyslop` (with space) — audit shell quoting at
